topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara traced the worn spine of her textbook, the fluorescent lights of the library humming a monotonous tune above her. The acceptance letter, pristine and promising, lay open on the table, a beacon of hope that suddenly felt brittle in her hands. She reread the fine print, the dedication to the scholarship fund, and a slow, heavy sinking began in her chest. It wasn't a sudden jolt, more like the slow, inevitable settling of silt. A quiet understanding bloomed—the entire edifice of her achievement was built upon a foundation that wasn't hers. She didn't move to call anyone, didn't even lift her gaze from the page. Instead, she carefully folded the paper, placing it back into the envelope as if sealing a secret she no longer wished to acknowledge. The world outside the library seemed distant, muffled, as if she were underwater.

[story 2]

Mark sat at his desk, the afternoon sun slanting across the dusty textbooks. He picked up the official notification, the embossed seal feeling cold against his fingertips. A strange hollowness expanded in his stomach, a sensation akin to a slow leak. He absorbed the information—the name listed, the specific criteria—and the energy seemed to drain from his limbs, leaving him feeling strangely weighted, anchored to his chair. He didn't argue. The words didn't sting; they just registered as facts, cold and undeniable. He let his shoulders slump, the tension draining away until he was simply still. He looked out the window at the campus quad, the vibrant activity of students seeming too loud, too demanding. He decided, very gently, that the best course of action was simply to wait, to let the situation settle into its new, quiet arrangement.

[story 3]

From the moment the email landed, Sarah felt a peculiar stillness descend. It wasn't panic; it was more like the sudden cessation of internal motion. She stared at the screen, the text blurring slightly, and felt a strange, almost pleasant numbness creeping into her extremities. The reality of the situation—the scholarship being allocated elsewhere—didn't provoke anger or tears. Instead, her breathing became shallow and even, a rhythmic sigh. She leaned back in her chair, the movement deliberate and unhurried, as if gravity had suddenly increased its hold on her. She picked up a pen, turned it over in her fingers, and watched the light catch the polished metal. Everything felt muted, softened, as if she were observing the world through a layer of fine, undisturbed dust.
